speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewer, James Alexander Bond. The city of New York, USA. In English, this is tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Auschwitz, at that time, was not known yet as a camp of horror and of industrialized extermination. But there had been rumors. And where these rumors came from no one knew, but they were there. And we lived by rumors, because we had no access to English or American or Allied newspapers, broadcast from the BBC. We only had the German version. But rumors kept flying all the time. And part of the rumors were Auschwitz was bad. Besides, it meant being brought deeper into German control, further away from the Allied armies who were in-- in Italy. If not within immediate reach, but at least there was hope that soon they would advance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got this idea of escaping. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the afternoon, already, my younger brother, Harry, had disappeared. How, I didn't know. But he came to me and he said, look, I'm going to try to get out. I said, how, how can you? I will try. If I don't come-- come back, tell father that I escaped. Now, all I remember, I saw him walk towards the exit of the stable where we were kept, and the German soldier there saw him coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he very, very slowly, playing with his little stone that he kept kicking and following. And he wiggled his way out of the stable without the German stopping him. That doesn't mean he was out. He was now in that huge courtyard surrounded all around by that wall. And German officers coming, going, soldiers, and that's when I lost track of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a few hours later he still hadn't reappeared and no German came in screaming, where's the family of this kid? So I assumed that somehow he had managed. OK. It's now evening. And we are told the train leaves for Auschwitz in the morning. And I got this here idea of escaping. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a very difficult moment there to face. Do I stay with my father? Or, do I try to save myself? Can I be-- he was incapable of even considering what I was trying to do because of his wound, which still hadn't healed, of course, and was getting worse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The quandary was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do I stay with him? Or, do I try to escape, so at least one of us is-- is saved? Can I change anything in his destiny, in the future, by staying with-- with him? I didn't have much time to consider all of that, but it was very much present on my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was driven by the desire to get out of the German's hands. And the only way I saw was to climb that wall. So I told father, look, I'm-- I'm going to try. There is absolutely no guarantee that I will succeed. And even, I may be worse off than before. But I will try. And I will try also to get some assistance to you, maybe through the priest, once I'm outside. Maybe they can get you out. OK. I asked to be included in the next toilet detail. We went, I squatted down. When all the others had finished, I remained. I figured that if he counts, the German sentry, he will see that one is missing. I can always say that I haven't finished yet. But I heard the steps receding. They were all gone. I was left alone. I ventured outside the cubicle. It was about 10 feet between the toilets and the wall. The only reason I could consider climbing over the wall was somehow just opposite the toilets, that part of the wall had some metal rungs stuck all along maybe left over from some repaired job, some masonry job. But they were there. And I dashed across [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and made my way slowly up. Now, this may sound incredible. But there was a full moon that-- that night. And above the stable were offices. And as I got higher, I could look into the windows of the offices. And I saw German soldiers there and women soldiers typing, working, office work coming, going.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to myself, oh, my god, how come nobody sees me? I kept climbing until I reached the four strands of barbed wire. Now, this was the most difficult part. Because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the barbed wire kept hooking itself into my clothes. And I had to take each strand over my hand-- over my head and reach for the next one until I was right on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once I was on top, straddling it, this side was the Garrison, here was the street on the sidewalk. At both ends were German sentries. But they kept walking back and forth. So I figured my best chance is to wait until they are as far away as possible from-- from me. Then I turned around and let myself hang on the outside, gaining this way, about 6 feet, instead of jumping directly. And I let go. Letting go, I scraped the inside of my wrist against the masonry. But I fell back on my back, picked myself up, and immediately crossed the street and hid into the entrance of-- of a building.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't have to tell you, my heart, my mind, everything was pounding, pounding, pounding. I couldn't believe that I was actually-- there is the garrison, and I'm free. I'm outside. Now, where do I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only place I knew was the convent where my mother was hidden. And I didn't really know how to get there from where I-- I-- I was, which I learned later was called La Zecca, Z-E-C-C-A, that garrison, right on the Arno river, the river of Florence. So, vaguely, I knew its in that direction. And I started walking expecting, any second now, some German motorcycle to come after me, sirens wailing. The commotion was all inside of-- of me, nobody came after me. I walked, it's quite a walk from La-- La Zecca to the Varlungo convent where I knew my mother was hidden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As I approached it, I thought, what if the Germans had raided that convent, too? And my mother, I either see her in the people they brought to the garrison, or she was brought to some other place. And I'm going to fall right into the hands of some Germans still stationed there. So I approached very carefully. And I didn't hear anything. It was quiet and calm. I rang the bell. I could hear it ringing inside. And it was quiet for quite a while until I heard foot-- footsteps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the door opened. And a priest pulled me in, and immediately closed the door behind me. And that was my first contact with Don Giovanni Semioni, who was the chaplain of that convent. Don Casini, whom I may have mentioned before, yes I did. Don Letu Casini was in charge of overall throughout Florence. Don Giovanni was in charge of that convent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He embraced me like a lost brother. Because he had heard that I was caught. He knew of the men that were hidden at the abandoned movie theater. And then he said, you can come on down now. He yelled, away from me, it's safe. And down came, jumping down from the tree, came my brother, Harry. He, too, managed to climb over the wall. But he couldn't jump, it was too high. I should give you an idea of how high it-- it-- it was. I never actually measured it. But it must have been anywhere between 12 and 15 feet, a very massive, thick wall, like they built fortresses, you know, at the time. It was too high. What he did is he saw an Italian riding by with a bicycle. And he whistled to-- to him, come near. And the guy, startled, got off his bike. He sees-- sees a young kid on top of a wall. Without explaining, Harry jumped, counting on the instinctive reflex of the guy to open his arms and catch him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he said, get me on the bike, get me out of here quick. He said, what? Why would I? I'll explain later, just get me out of here, which he did. And he also made his way to Varlungo. So now we are reunited. But my mother inside does not know anything of that yet. So we decided to let her have another night's sleep together with the other women, who all of them had their husbands, brothers, or sons also with us, the men who were caught at the cinema. So the next morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were brought to my mother's presence. And just looking at us, she knew immediately. And we said, look, we can consider us luck-- luckier than most, because both of us escaped. Out of all the people in that garrison, the two of us escaped. And maybe father will be equally lucky being that he is sick, maybe they will treat him gently.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also had asked Don Giovanni that next morning to go immediately to the garrison and ask to speak to my father, counting on the appearance of a clergyman. And if that would not succeed, I give him a little note, telling my father both his sons are safe and wrap it in a stone. And I told him at which part of the wall, behind which part of the wall, the stable was where my father and we were. So that he could throw it over, maybe somebody will find it. He told us later that he went, but the train to Auschwitz had already left. It left something like 6 o'clock in the morning. Now, immediately my brother and I were brought-- transferred from the convent, where no men were allowed in, only women-- to a Catholic orphanage in another part of Florence, called Rifredi. And the orphanage was called Madonnina Del Grappa, under the direction of Don Giulio Facibeni. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were brought there and Don Giovanni accompanied us to the orphanage. And we were handed over. We were brought in to see Don Facibeni, the director. And only he and his assistant, Don Nello, knew we were Jews. We concocted a whole story, because we're going to be mingled with the other children who were all Catholic. And the story was that we were children of Italian parents caught behind British lines. My mother. The father is Italian, but on the Russian front somewhere. We don't speak Italian, because we were born in France and raised in-- in France. And that's why French was speaking-- spoken at-- spoken at home. And that's why we don't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] speak Italian fluently. We were given fake names immediately, Capaldi. I became Nicola Capaldi. My brother was Francesco. And there was one other boy there, Jewish boy, who also had been caught at the cinema. But he managed, in the confusion and in the hysteria of the moment, to quickly make his way up to the balcony where the seats had been stashed. And he hid under the seats. And he waited till everybody was gone, and then found his way, also, to the orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, there the three of us, all Capaldis, all related, all with the same story. And we started living in that orphanage all through-- we're now November, the day after the raid, November 7, 1943-- all through until the liberation of Florence. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were never compromised, mainly because Don Giovanni, after having secured a safe place in that orphanage, went back and took care of all the other Jewish refugees who were-- who had escaped this raid, who had managed not to be caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the reason he was in charge now was that Don Casini, the original supervisor of all these clandestine operation, had been seized by the Gestapo and was held at the Murate prison in Florence. So that Don Giovanni took over the responsibility, which was enormous. There were still hundreds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he came to visit us occasionally, always inquiring about our well-being. While in-- at the Varlungo convent, he was concerned with the women that were hidden there. And he was advised by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the archbishop that things were getting very tough in-- in-- in Florence. They had their informers. And they knew that the Germans had their eye on him, Don Giovanni. Mind you, he was all of maybe 25 years old at-- at the time. And he was in Florence, because he was studying at the University there in addition of being chaplain at the convent. He was studying philosophy, history, et cetera. But that was not his birthplace. His birthplace was in the north of Italy, a town called Treviso, north of Venice. But he found himself in Florence during these events. But he kept taking care of all the Jews that he had been entrusted with, who before had been handled by Don Casini.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the situation got worse-- because the German raids continued, not just for that one massive one that blanketed all of Florence. They went on continuously on a lesser scale. He decided his Jewish women are not safe enough. What if they invade the convent itself, which they did? In other convents people had been caught, the nuns taken prisoners, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: He hit upon a radical solution. In the garden, behind the convent, there was an underground cave where they kept various gardening tools, and plants, and seeds, and bags of fertilizer, and so forth. All of this was removed, cleaned away, straw was brought in, and 12 women and children, girls, were brought to live in that cave. The entrance was hidden by flower pots and so forth. Twice a day, a nun came with a special signal on the door. The flower pots were removed. She brought in some soup, some food, and removed the urine pot. This went on for quite a while until the archbishop told Don Giovanni that things are getting much too dangerous. Don Casini was still held prisoner by the Germans. If they catch him, Don Giovanni, it would compromise the entire Catholic institutions in France. And he ordered him to leave town. Now, Don Giovanni was considering leaving town. But he couldn't, or didn't want to, abandon his women. He decided to transfer them all with him to his city of birth, Treviso, in the north. But he couldn't very well take all 12 at the same time. You must remember that at that time Italy was in the middle of a serious war, meaning daily bombardments on train tracks, train stations, cities, towns, and roads, plus, fascist brigades all over. So the idea was to attract as little attention as possible. And he decided to take them two or three at a-- at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he made four trips from Florence to Treviso, back to Florence, back to Treviso, each time taking two or three. There's an amusing incident connected with this. Because he was under surveillance, at one time he decided to go in civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: These priests were dressed, not like you see them here, in long one piece black cassocks, with 33 buttons all the way down-- 33 being the age of Christ at which he died-- very severe attire, with a flat black hat, large rim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So since he was under a surveillance, he decided to go in civilian clothes. On that trip, he took with him a lady that was pregnant. Her husband had been caught with us and departed with my father. And she had a small child, Harry [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was her name at the time, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they traveled all this trip from Florence to Treviso, where they arrived. And this was not one continuous trip. It was interrupted along the way, because of bombardments, rails had been destroyed. And they had to resume the-- the trip a few kilometers on. It was quite an ordeal. They finally arrived in Treviso at the home of his mother. Now, you can imagine the reaction of the mother, who knows her son is a priest arriving in the middle of the night in civilian clothes with a pregnant woman in tow. Right? [LAUGHTER] So he had to reassure her. He said, mom, mom. It's nothing like what you think, et cetera. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: While all this was going on, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arriving in Treviso, he entrusted, while he went back and forth back to Florence, he entrusted the woman to a seminary friend of-- of his. They had been schooled together. They graduated together. They became priests ordained together, Don Angelo De La Torre. Now, while Don Giovanni was gone, Don Angelo De La Torre arranged for all these women to be hidden, again, in small groups, here and there, institutions, convents, monasteries, whatever. When Don Giovanni returned to Florence, he-- as I said, he visited us occasionally. And one time, he brought us fake Italian identity cards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did he get those? In Treviso. In Treviso, which was his hometown, he knew a lot of people, families. And a lot of these families had their sons in the underground. Partisans, they were called.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this wasn't openly spoken about. But those who knew, knew. Through them, Don Giovanni got in touch with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one organization that arranged for fake identity cards. Now, Don Giovanni got the blanks. And he sat in his room typing up the story, imagined fabricated stories, names, places of birth, date of birth, of every Jew that he had under his surveillance, under his protection. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then he gave the cards back, which were now blanks, blanks filled out, but who needed that special stamp, that embossed stamp. He gave it back to the underground. The cards came back a little while later, stamped. He now faked the signature of the-- of the commissioner of police. And he handed us, in Florence, those cards, like he handed them over to all the women hidden in Treviso. Now, this card becomes of special importance. Slowly, the allied armies inched their way up the Italian peninsula. They where still very far away from Florence. But you may have heard of Monte Cassino, which is a major, major blocking of the Allied troops. It was a monastery, sitting on top of a hill, commanding the view of the entire valley, blocking any advance of the Allied armies. And although the monastery itself had been the subject of constant heavy bombardments, the Germans still were deeply entrenched until the day they broke through. And now, the Allied armies slowly inched their ways up. As they approached Florence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans became more and more vicious. Not just because they had to retreat, but there were also partisan sniper attacks on-- on them. And one day, the whole orphanage-- which was a huge complex, it had classrooms. I think there were about 250 children there at the time. It had classrooms. It had workshops for tailoring, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shoes repairs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] woodworking, metalworking. It was a whole organization, not just a small institute. The entire orphanage was surrounded by German troops. And they burst into our room where I and Willy was hidden. My brother, Harry, had in the meantime been transferred north to Treviso. So there were just the-- just the two of us. They burst in. And I understood German. And I hear one of the two say to the other, only take the one that's over 18 years old. Now, Willy had a false card. And I had a false-- false card. But I was a year older and looking older than Willy. But Don Giovanni, when he made those cards, had the wisdom to make us a year younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So although, I was 18 at the time, the card said 17. But now, they take me. And they bring me down in the courtyard, lined up with another group of about, I would say eight or nine young men, who used to be orphans at the orphanage, but who since then had gone out into the world and come back now. Which, I learned later, they were in the underground. So now, I'm lined up against the wall with these other kids. And I'm saying to myself, now, I heard them say only those over 18. Now, what if I make a fuss? He will ask, how come you-- you-- you know about this business of 18? It means I speak German. How come you speak German, et cetera? So I figured, well, let's see first what they want to do with us. So we're lined up against the wall and comes a group of soldiers. I told you, the entire area was surrounded, blocked, a group of two or three soldiers, one of them with a machine gun. An officer comes, talks to them. They look at us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you will not believe this, because a similar incident happens in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The guy with the machine gun lifts it, looks at us, and pulls the trigger. And nothing happens, jammed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They work at it. They take out the magazine. They reinsert it. They fumble around. It doesn't work. They look at us again. They confer. OK. One of the Germans comes towards us. And he said, you and you, meaning me and another guy, come with-- come with-- come with us, actually. There were two. And we were taken away, walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, all this-- this time, I'm saying to myself, but if I'm only 17, I shouldn't be here. Something's wrong. But how do I do that without giving myself away?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were led out of Florence, out of the orphanage, going north for about 2 miles until we reached a farmhouse. And that farmhouse had become the concentration place of all the young men and men they caught in that area. And we are brought to a room. And an officer comes-- comes in. And he says, well, it's about time you make yourself useful. You're going to be brought to Germany to work in armaments factory. So at this time, I had a sigh of relief. No one knows that I'm Jew yet. But now, again, I said, this is the decisive moment. And the trick in those situations always is when to judge the decisive moment has arrived. You wait too long, it's too late. You speak up too soon, too-- too dangerous.
